    "text": "v0.6.10. Once the average pigment per cell crosses `AI_WATER_THRESHOLD = 0.75`, the AI planner stops drawing from the weighted strategy mix and exclusively picks the soften (water-brush) strategy until the average drops back below the threshold. Same brush footprint, same path generation, same curve characteristics \u2014 just the pigment swapped to `WATER_INDEX` and the brush size range bumped a bit (40-100px, vs the 20-60px of `mark`).\n\nA few design notes:\n\n**It's dynamic, not a one-way trigger.** I made it a per-stroke check rather than a latched state, so if the average later drops below 0.75 (you hit Lift, or Reset), the AI returns to its normal weighted mix. If you'd rather it stay water-only once tripped \u2014 until reset \u2014 that's a one-line change.\n\n**Why water doesn't bring the bar back down on its own:** the water brush adds water and lifts deposited pigment back into suspension (`d` \u2192 `g`), but doesn't subtract pigment. So total pigment stays constant under AI water-painting. The average doesn't fall on its own \u2014 it only falls if you use Lift, run a really wide Water brush near the canvas edges and push pigment off, or hit Reset.\n\n**Combined with edge darkening, this can do interesting things.** Once the AI flips to water-only, it'll keep pushing pressure into wet regions. Edge darkening continues running. So you'll see pigment migrating to the edges of the existing painted regions \u2014 the \"tide-line\" behavior that real watercolor does on saturated paper. The painting won't change in total pigment content, but it will keep visually evolving until things dry out.\n\n**The threshold is one constant.** `AI_WATER_THRESHOLD = 0.75` sits right next to the AI state declarations. If 0.75 turns out to be too high or too low in practice, that's the only knob.",
    "citations": []
  }

    "type": "text",
    "text": "v0.6.11. Three changes work together:\n\n**CSS.** Canvas changed from `inset: 0` (fills viewport exactly) to `inset: -2.5vh -2.5vw` (extends 2.5% past every edge). The body's existing `overflow: hidden` clips the hidden margin. Total canvas size is now 105vw \u00d7 105vh, with the visible viewport showing only the middle 100vw \u00d7 100vh.\n\n**Grid sizing.** I introduced `CANVAS_OVERSCAN = 1.05` and multiplied the grid dimensions by it. This was the important coordinated change: if I'd only resized the CSS without growing the grid, the same cell count would just be stretched across 5% more pixels, and the artifacts would still be visible (just slightly compressed). With the grid resized too, each cell still maps to `SCALE` display pixels \u2014 so the cells stay the same physical size and the extra ~10% of cells live entirely in the hidden margin, doing the simulation work that creates the visible edges' clean look.\n\n**Cursor positioning.** This is the subtle one. The cursor element is `position: fixed`, so its `left`/`top` are interpreted as viewport coordinates. When the canvas was at `inset: 0`, canvas-relative coordinates equaled viewport coordinates by coincidence. With the canvas extended, `rect.left = -2.5vw_in_px` (negative), so `clientX - rect.left` is no longer equal to `clientX` \u2014 it's offset by 2.5vw. The cursor would drift 2.5vw to the right of where the pointer actually is. Fix: `clientToCanvas` now returns `e.clientX` and `e.clientY` directly for the cursor, while the grid coordinate calculation (which correctly uses `(clientX - rect.left) / rect.width`) is unchanged and still produces correct sim coordinates.\n\n**Perf note.** The grid now has ~10% more cells (5% in each dimension, compounding). At SCALE=2 this is a noticeable bump in per-frame cost. If you ever revisit SCALE, the overscan factor will scale the cost proportionally.\n\n**Pigment readout caveat.** The average pigment per cell will read slightly lower than before for the same painting effort, because the same painted area now divides by ~10% more total cells. If you want to keep the pre-overscan calibration, `PIGMENT_REFERENCE` could be bumped from 0.3 to roughly 0.286 to compensate \u2014 but honestly the change is small enough that I'd leave it alone unless it bothers you.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

I'll set it to true whenever paint is applied, then keep it active only while the simulation has meaningful changes\u2014wet hasn't fully evaporated and suspended pigment is still moving. Once everything settles below a threshold for a few frames, I can pause the simulation entirely.\n\nTo detect when the sim is truly idle, I need three conditions: no water flow (max velocity near zero), no water left (max wet below threshold), and no suspended pigment settling. Rather than checking every cell each frame, I'll track these as running totals or sample infrequently to avoid overhead.\n\nFor the bigger win, I'm focusing on sparse activity tracking\u2014most cells are just dry paper most of the time, so iterating only over active tiles would cut the workload significantly.\n\nI'm considering a tile-based approach where I divide the grid into chunks, mark which tiles have activity, and only process those. The challenge is restructuring the current linear array operations to work tile-by-tile. A simpler alternative is tracking just the active row range, though that only helps with vertical localization. For JavaScript performance, the cleanest solution is maintaining an array of active tile indices that the sim operations iterate through.\n\nThe tricky part is that diffusion and advection need to read neighboring cells, so I'd need to include a one-tile buffer around painted regions in the active list. Given realistic time constraints, I'm prioritizing three quick wins: an idle-skip flag to avoid simulation and rendering when nothing's happening, decoupling the sim rate from the display rate with fixed timesteps, and making the render fast-path more aggressive to skip work on nearly-empty cells.\n\nFor a simpler optimization than full tile tracking, I could maintain a bounding box of the active region\u2014any cell with meaningful wetness or pigment\u2014and only iterate within that during simulation.         "type": "text",
        "text": "   668\tfunction movePigment() {\n   669\t  for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) g_tmp[k].set(g[k]);\n   670\t\n   671\t  for (let y = 1; y < GH - 1; y++) {\n   672\t    const yo = y * GW;\n   673\t    for (let x = 1; x < GW - 1; x++) {\n   674\t      const i = yo + x;\n   675\t      if (wet[i] < 0.04) continue;\n   676\t      const ux = u[i], vy = v[i];\n   677\t      const adt = DT * 0.7;\n   678\t\n   679\t      for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n   680\t        const gk = g[k][i];\n   681\t        if (gk < 0.0008) continue;\n   682\t        let outFlux = 0;\n   683\t        if (ux > 0) {\n   684\t          const f = ux * gk * adt;\n   685\t          g_tmp[k][i + 1] += f; outFlux += f;\n   686\t        } else if (ux < 0) {\n   687\t          const f = -ux * gk * adt;\n   688\t          g_tmp[k][i - 1] += f; outFlux += f;\n   689\t        }\n   690\t        if (vy > 0) {\n   691\t          const f = vy * gk * adt;\n   692\t          g_tmp[k][i + GW] += f; outFlux += f;\n   693\t        } else if (vy < 0) {\n   694\t          const f = -vy * gk * adt;\n   695\t          g_tmp[k][i - GW] += f; outFlux += f;\n   696\t        }\n   697\t        g_tmp[k][i] -= outFlux;\n   698\t      }\n   699\t    }\n   700\t  }\n   701\t  for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n   702\t    const arr = g[k];\n   703\t    const src = g_tmp[k];\n   704\t    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n   705\t      const vv = src[i];\n   706\t      arr[i] = vv > MAX_PIGMENT ? MAX_PIGMENT : vv;\n   707\t    }\n   708\t  }\n   709\t\n   710\t  // Isotropic diffusion (smooths sharp pixel artifacts, gives soft wet-in-wet feel)\n   711\t  for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n   712\t    const arr = g[k];\n   713\t    const tmp = g_tmp[k];\n   714\t    tmp.set(arr);\n   715\t    for (let y = 1; y < GH - 1; y++) {\n   716\t      const yo = y * GW;\n   717\t      for (let x = 1; x < GW - 1; x++) {\n   718\t        const i = yo + x;\n   719\t        if (wet[i] < 0.04) continue;\n   720\t        const lap = tmp[i - 1] + tmp[i + 1] + tmp[i - GW] + tmp[i + GW] - 4 * tmp[i];\n   721\t        arr[i] = tmp[i] + PIGMENT_DIFFUSION * lap;\n   722\t      }\n   723\t    }\n   724\t  }\n   725\t}\n   726\t\n   727\t// ============================================================\n   728\t// TRANSFER PIGMENT (\u00a74.5) \u2014 adsorption / desorption with granulation \u03b3\n   729\t// ============================================================\n   730\tfunction transferPigment() {\n   731\t  for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n   732\t    const pig = PIGMENTS[k];\n   733\t    const dens = pig.density;\n   734\t    const stain = pig.staining;\n   735\t    const gran = pig.granulation;\n   736\t    const gk = g[k], dk = d[k];\n   737\t    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n   738\t      if (wet[i] < 0.04) continue;\n   739\t      const h = paperH[i];\n   740\t      const gi = gk[i], di = dk[i];\n   741\t      let down = gi * (1 - h * gran) * dens;\n   742\t      let up   = di * (1 + (h - 1) * gran) * dens / stain;\n   743\t      if (down < 0) down = 0;\n   744\t      if (up   < 0) up   = 0;\n   745\t      if (di + down > 1) down = Math.max(0, 1 - di);\n   746\t      if (gi + up   > 1) up   = Math.max(0, 1 - gi);\n   747\t      dk[i] = di + down - up;\n   748\t      gk[i] = gi + up   - down;\n   749\t    }\n   750\t  }\n   751\t}\n   752\t\n   753\t// ============================================================\n   754\t// EVAPORATION \u2014 water slowly leaves the paper\n   755\t// When a cell goes dry, any suspended pigment locks in as deposited.\n   756\t// ============================================================\n   757\tfunction evaporate() {\n   758\t  for (let i = 0; MAX_PIGMENT : vv;\\n    }\\n  }\\n\\n  // Isotropic diffusion (smooths sharp pixel artifacts, gives soft wet-in-wet feel)\\n  for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\\n    const arr = g[k];\\n    const tmp = g_tmp[k];\\n    tmp.set(arr);\\n    for (let y = 1; y < GH - 1; y++) {\\n      const yo = y * GW;\\n      for (let x = 1; x < GW - 1; x++) {\\n        const i = yo + x;\\n        if (wet[i] < 0.04) continue;\\n        const lap = tmp[i - 1] + tmp[i + 1] + tmp[i - GW] + tmp[i + GW] - 4 * tmp[i];\\n        arr[i] = tmp[i] + PIGMENT_DIFFUSION * lap;\\n      }\\n    }\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n// ============================================================\\n// TRANSFER PIGMENT (\\u00a74.5) \\u2014 adsorption / desorption with granulation \\u03b3\\n// ============================================================\\nfunction transferPigment() {\\n  for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\\n    const pig = PIGMENTS[k];\\n    const dens = pig.density;\\n    const stain = pig.staining;\\n    const gran = pig.granulation;\\n    const gk = g[k], dk = d[k];\\n    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\\n      if (wet[i] < 0.04) continue;\\n      const h = paperH[i];\\n      const gi = gk[i], di = dk[i];\\n      let down = gi * (1 - h * gran) * dens;\\n      let up   = di * (1 + (h - 1) * gran) * dens / stain;\\n      if (down < 0) down = 0;\\n      if (up   < 0) up   = 0;\\n      if (di + down > 1) down = Math.max(0, 1 - di);\\n      if (gi + up   > 1) up   = Math.max(0, 1 - gi);\\n      dk[i] = di + down - up;\\n      gk[i] = gi + up   - down;\\n    }\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n// ============================================================\\n// EVAPORATION \\u2014 water slowly leaves the paper\\n// When a cell goes dry, any suspended pigment locks in as deposited.\\n// ============================================================\\nfunction evaporate() {\\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\\n    let w = wet[i] * 0.9988;\\n    if (w < 0.025) {\\n      // dump suspended -> deposited\\n      for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\\n        let nd = d[k][i] + g[k][i];\\n        if (nd > MAX_PIGMENT) nd = MAX_PIGMENT;\\n        d[k][i] = nd;\\n        g[k][i] = 0;\\n      }\\n      w = 0;\\n      u[i] = 0; v[i] = 0;\\n    }\\n    wet[i] = w;\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n// ============================================================\\n// SIM STEP\\n// Two toggles (see UI wiring below) gate the relevant sub-steps:\\n//   edgeDarkeningEnabled \\u2014 when off, the \\u00a74.3.3 outward-flow pressure\\n//     reduction is skipped entirely. The vertical blur pass is even worse\u2014it's doing strided memory access that wastes cachelines, and the min/max clamping in the inner loop compiles to branches that add up across millions of iterations. Pressure decay is another full pass that could be folded into the velocity update instead of running separately.\n\nThe render function is calling expensive math operations like sqrt and exp for every channel on every cell, even when pigment values are negligible\u2014I could use lookup tables or skip low-pigment cells more aggressively. The idle case is wasteful too: the simulation keeps running every frame even when nothing's happening. And simStep runs twice per frame for visual liveliness, but decoupling the sim rate would let me run it once at a fixed 30Hz instead of doubling the cost. The bar updates are iterating over all cells every frame when they could update less frequently.\n\nFor v0.7, I'm planning a tiered approach.     "input": {
      "description": "Optimize boxBlur. The horizontal pass splits into 3 regions \u2014 left edge (where the subtract index would underflow), middle (no clamps needed, the hot 95% of iterations), and right edge (where the add index would overflow). The vertical pass gets the same treatment. The math is identical to before \u2014 just eliminating predictable branches that were running on every inner iteration. The single-pixel index lookups within each region are now branchless typed-array reads, which V8 turns into much tighter code.",
      "new_str": "function boxBlur(src, dst, radius) {\n  const inv = 1 / (2 * radius + 1);\n  const r = radius;\n  const gw = GW, gh = GH;\n  const tmp = wetBlurTmp;\n\n  // ---- HORIZONTAL PASS: src -> wetBlurTmp ----\n  // Each row's inner loop is split into three regions so the clamps\n  // can be hoisted out of the hot middle iteration. Middle covers the\n  // vast majority of cells \u2014 the edges are only `radius` wide each.\n  for (let y = 0; y < gh; y++) {\n    const yo = y * gw;\n    let sum = 0;\n    // Initialize the window: -r..+r, clamped to row bounds\n    for (let xx = -r; xx <= r; xx++) {\n      const ix = xx < 0 ? 0 : xx > gw - 1 ? gw - 1 : xx;\n      sum += src[yo + ix];\n    }\n    // Left region: x < r, \"sub\" side clamps to 0\n    let x = 0;\n    const leftEnd = r < gw ? r : gw;\n    while (x < leftEnd) {\n      tmp[yo + x] = sum * inv;\n      const addX = x + r + 1;\n      sum += src[yo + (addX > gw - 1 ? gw - 1 : addX)];\n      sum -= src[yo]; // sub clamps to 0\n      x++;\n    }\n    // Middle: both indices in bounds \u2014 no clamps\n    const midEnd = gw - r - 1 > 0 ? gw - r - 1 : 0;\n    while (x < midEnd) {\n      tmp[yo + x] = sum * inv;\n      sum += src[yo + x + r + 1] - src[yo + x - r];\n      x++;\n    }\n    // Right region: \"add\" side clamps to gw - 1\n    const lastIdx = yo + gw - 1;\n    while (x < gw) {\n      tmp[yo + x] = sum * inv;\n      const subX = x - r;\n      sum += src[lastIdx] - src[yo + (subX < 0 ? 0 : subX)];\n      x++;\n    }\n  }\n\n  // ---- VERTICAL PASS: wetBlurTmp -> dst ----\n  // Same 3-region split. Note this pass strides by gw between reads,\n  // so it's less cache-friendly than the horizontal pass; the clamp\n  // elimination is the cheap win we can take here without restructuring\n  // memory layout.\n  for (let x = 0; x < gw; x++) {\n    let sum = 0;\n    for (let yy = -r; yy <= r; yy++) {\n      const iy = yy < 0 ? 0 : yy > gh - 1 ? gh - 1 : yy;\n      sum += tmp[iy * gw + x];\n    }\n    let y = 0;\n    const topEnd = r < gh ? r : gh;\n    while (y < topEnd) {\n      dst[y * gw + x] = sum * inv;\n      const addY = y + r + 1;\n      sum += tmp[(addY > gh - 1 ? gh - 1 : addY) * gw + x];\n      sum -= tmp[x]; // sub clamps to row 0\n      y++;\n    }\n    const midEnd = gh - r - 1 > 0 ? gh - r - 1 : 0;\n    while (y < midEnd) {\n      dst[y * gw + x] = sum * inv;\n      sum += tmp[(y + r + 1) * gw + x] - tmp[(y - r) * gw + x];\n      y++;\n    }\n    const lastRowOff = (gh - 1) * gw;\n    while (y < gh) {\n      dst[y * gw + x] = sum * inv;\n      const subY = y - r;\n      sum += tmp[lastRowOff + x] - tmp[(subY < 0 ? 0 : subY) * gw + x];\n      y++;\n    }\n  }\n}",
      "old_str": "function boxBlur(src, dst, radius) {\n  const inv = 1 / (2 * radius + 1);\n  // horizontal pass: src -> wetBlurTmp\n  for (let y = 0; y < GH; y++) {\n    let sum = 0;\n    for (let x = -radius; x <= radius; x++) {\n      sum += src[y * GW + Math.max(0, Math.min(GW - 1, x))];\n    }\n    for (let x = 0; x < GW; x++) {\n      wetBlurTmp[y * GW + x] = sum * inv;\n      const add = src[y * GW + Math.min(GW - 1, x + radius + 1)];\n      const sub = src[y * GW + Math.max(0, x - radius)];\n      sum += add - sub;\n    }\n  }\n  // vertical pass: wetBlurTmp -> dst\n  for (let x = 0; x < GW; x++) {\n    let sum = 0;\n    for (let y = -radius; y <= radius; y++) {\n      sum += wetBlurTmp[Math.max(0, Math.min(GH - 1, y)) * GW + x];\n    }\n    for (let y = 0; y < GH; y++) {\n      dst[y * GW + x] = sum * inv;\n      const add = wetBlurTmp[Math.min(GH - 1, y + radius + 1) * GW + x];\n      const sub = wetBlurTmp[Math.max(0, y - radius) * GW + x];\n      sum += add - sub;\n    }\n  }\n}",

      "new_str": "  // Isotropic diffusion (smooths sharp pixel artifacts, gives soft wet-in-wet feel)\n  // Fused single pass: all 3 pigments handled inline per cell, so we\n  // pay the loop overhead and the wet[i] check once instead of 3x.\n  // Same Laplacian math as before, applied to each pigment.\n  const tmp0 = g_tmp[0], tmp1 = g_tmp[1], tmp2 = g_tmp[2];\n  const arr0 = g[0], arr1 = g[1], arr2 = g[2];\n  tmp0.set(arr0); tmp1.set(arr1); tmp2.set(arr2);\n  const PD = PIGMENT_DIFFUSION;\n  for (let y = 1; y < GH - 1; y++) {\n    const yo = y * GW;\n    for (let x = 1; x < GW - 1; x++) {\n      const i = yo + x;\n      if (wet[i] < 0.04) continue;\n      const im1 = i - 1, ip1 = i + 1, im_g = i - GW, ip_g = i + GW;\n      const c0 = tmp0[i], c1 = tmp1[i], c2 = tmp2[i];\n      arr0[i] = c0 + PD * (tmp0[im1] + tmp0[ip1] + tmp0[im_g] + tmp0[ip_g] - 4 * c0);\n      arr1[i] = c1 + PD * (tmp1[im1] + tmp1[ip1] + tmp1[im_g] + tmp1[ip_g] - 4 * c1);\n      arr2[i] = c2 + PD * (tmp2[im1] + tmp2[ip1] + tmp2[im_g] + tmp2[ip_g] - 4 * c2);\n    }\n  }\n}",

      "new_str": "function transferPigment() {\n  // Fused single pass: 3 pigments handled inline per cell.\n  // The pigment-specific constants are hoisted to locals so V8 can keep\n  // them in registers across the inner loop instead of refetching from\n  // the PIGMENTS array on every iteration.\n  const p0 = PIGMENTS[0], p1 = PIGMENTS[1], p2 = PIGMENTS[2];\n  const den0 = p0.density, sta0 = p0.staining, gra0 = p0.granulation;\n  const den1 = p1.density, sta1 = p1.staining, gra1 = p1.granulation;\n  const den2 = p2.density, sta2 = p2.staining, gra2 = p2.granulation;\n  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n  const d0 = d[0], d1 = d[1], d2 = d[2];\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    if (wet[i] < 0.04) continue;\n    const h = paperH[i];\n    const hg0 = 1 - h * gra0, hg1 = 1 - h * gra1, hg2 = 1 - h * gra2;\n    const hu0 = 1 + (h - 1) * gra0, hu1 = 1 + (h - 1) * gra1, hu2 = 1 + (h - 1) * gra2;\n\n    // pigment 0\n    let gi = g0[i], di = d0[i];\n    let down = gi * hg0 * den0;\n    let up   = di * hu0 * den0 / sta0;\n    if (down < 0) down = 0;\n    if (up   < 0) up   = 0;\n    if (di + down > 1) down = 1 - di > 0 ? 1 - di : 0;\n    if (gi + up   > 1) up   = 1 - gi > 0 ? 1 - gi : 0;\n    d0[i] = di + down - up;\n    g0[i] = gi + up   - down;\n\n    // pigment 1\n    gi = g1[i]; di = d1[i];\n    down = gi * hg1 * den1;\n    up   = di * hu1 * den1 / sta1;\n    if (down < 0) down = 0;\n    if (up   < 0) up   = 0;\n    if (di + down > 1) down = 1 - di > 0 ? 1 - di : 0;\n    if (gi + up   > 1) up   = 1 - gi > 0 ? 1 - gi : 0;\n    d1[i] = di + down - up;\n    g1[i] = gi + up   - down;\n\n    // pigment 2\n    gi = g2[i]; di = d2[i];\n    down = gi * hg2 * den2;\n    up   = di * hu2 * den2 / sta2;\n    if (down < 0) down = 0;\n    if (up   < 0) up   = 0;\n    if (di + down > 1) down = 1 - di > 0 ? 1 - di : 0;\n    if (gi + up   > 1) up   = 1 - gi > 0 ? 1 - gi : 0;\n    d2[i] = di + down - up;\n    g2[i] = gi + up   - down;\n  }\n}",
      "old_str": "function transferPigment() {\n  for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n    const pig = PIGMENTS[k];\n    const dens = pig.density;\n    const stain = pig.staining;\n    const gran = pig.granulation;\n    const gk = g[k], dk = d[k];\n    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n      if (wet[i] < 0.04) continue;\n      const h = paperH[i];\n      const gi = gk[i], di = dk[i];\n      let down = gi * (1 - h * gran) * dens;\n      let up   = di * (1 + (h - 1) * gran) * dens / stain;\n      if (down < 0) down = 0;\n      if (up   < 0) up   = 0;\n      if (di + down > 1) down = Math.max(0, 1 - di);\n      if (gi + up   > 1) up   = Math.max(0, 1 - gi);\n      dk[i] = di + down - up;\n      gk[i] = gi + up   - down;\n    }\n  }\n}",

      "new_str": "// Dryness indicator \u2014 average wet across the canvas, normalized against\n// the initial 0.62 baseline, clamped to [0, 1]. Updates the bar fill width.\nconst drynessFill = document.getElementById('dryness-fill');\nconst DRYNESS_REFERENCE = 0.62;\n\n// Pigment indicator \u2014 average total pigment per cell (summing suspended\n// + deposited across all three pigments), normalized against a target\n// \"substantially painted\" reference. Empty canvas reads as 0%, a heavily\n// painted canvas pins at 100%. Tuned to feel responsive during normal\n// painting rather than chasing the theoretical 3.0/cell saturation.\nconst pigmentFill = document.getElementById('pigment-fill');\nconst pigmentReadout = document.getElementById('pigment-readout');\nconst PIGMENT_REFERENCE = 0.3;\n\n// Cached totals from the most recent bar update. The idle-skip logic\n// reads these (O(1)) instead of re-iterating all N cells.\nlet lastTotalWet = 0;\nlet lastTotalSuspended = 0;\n\nfunction updateBars() {\n  // Fused pass: compute everything the bars and the idle check need\n  // in a single iteration over N. Previously this was two separate\n  // O(N) loops (updateDrynessBar + updatePigmentBar); merging them\n  // halves the bar-update cost and keeps wet[i] / g[k][i] / d[k][i]\n  // each touched once per frame instead of twice.\n  const g0 = g[0], g1 = g[1], g2 = g[2];\n  const d0 = d[0], d1 = d[1], d2 = d[2];\n  let tWet = 0, tSusp = 0, tPig = 0;\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    tWet += wet[i];\n    const s = g0[i] + g1[i] + g2[i];\n    tSusp += s;\n    tPig += s + d0[i] + d1[i] + d2[i];\n  }\n  lastTotalWet = tWet;\n  lastTotalSuspended = tSusp;\n\n  // --- Dryness ---\n  const avgWet = tWet / N;\n  let dry = 1 - avgWet / DRYNESS_REFERENCE;\n  if (dry < 0) dry = 0; else if (dry > 1) dry = 1;\n  drynessFill.style.width = (dry * 100).toFixed(1) + '%';\n\n  // --- Pigment ---\n  const avgPig = tPig / N;\n  // Expose to the AI planner so it can switch behavior on saturation\n  currentAvgPigment = avgPig;\n  let frac = avgPig / PIGMENT_REFERENCE;\n  if (frac < 0) frac = 0; else if (frac > 1) frac = 1;\n  pigmentFill.style.width = (frac * 100).toFixed(1) + '%';\n  // Readout shows raw average pigment per cell. Theoretical max is 3.0\n  // (all three pigments saturated everywhere); in practice values\n  // above ~1.0 indicate very heavy coverage.\n  pigmentReadout.textContent = avgPig.toFixed(3);\n}",

      "description": "Replace the loop with an idle-skip aware version. Three pieces of state: framesSincePaint (1 sec grace after every paint), simIsIdle (cached idle bit), framesSinceIdleCheck (rate-limits the cheap idle test). The check itself just reads cached totals from updateBars \u2014 so it's O(1) per frame. When idle, we skip simStep and render entirely. updateBars still runs (the bars do animate after sim stops while CSS transitions complete). The 'markCanvasActive' helper is exported for paintAt / rewet / resetSim to call so they can wake the sim cleanly.",
      "new_str": "// ============================================================\n// IDLE SKIP \u2014 sim/render skipping when canvas is settled\n// ============================================================\n// Most of a typical session, nothing is happening: the canvas is dry,\n// no pigment is moving, no input is arriving. Running simStep + render\n// every frame in that state burns CPU for no visible change. This\n// state machine detects when the sim has truly settled and skips the\n// hot path until something changes.\n//\n// Wake conditions (anything that mutates state must call markCanvasActive):\n//   \u2022 paintAt   \u2014 user or AI applies paint\n//   \u2022 rewet     \u2014 global re-wet button\n//   \u2022 resetSim  \u2014 Reset button\n//\n// Sleep conditions (all checked together once framesSincePaint > grace):\n//   \u2022 Average wet per cell below a small threshold\n//   \u2022 Average suspended pigment per cell below a small threshold\n// Deposited pigment (d[]) is dry and won't change without water input,\n// so we don't need it in the idle check.\n//\n// The totals lastTotalWet / lastTotalSuspended are maintained by\n// updateBars (which runs every frame), so the idle check itself is O(1).\n\nlet framesSincePaint = 9999;          // start \"settled\" \u2014 first paint wakes\nlet framesSinceIdleCheck = 0;\nlet simIsIdle = false;                // start active so initial state renders\nconst PAINT_GRACE_FRAMES = 60;        // 1 sec of forced sim after every paint\nconst IDLE_CHECK_INTERVAL = 30;\nconst IDLE_WET_PER_CELL = 0.025;      // matches evaporate's dry-out threshold\nconst IDLE_SUSPENDED_PER_CELL = 0.0005;\n\nfunction markCanvasActive() {\n  framesSincePaint = 0;\n  simIsIdle = false;\n}\n\nfunction shouldRunSim() {\n  // Always run during the grace period after any paint event \u2014 gives\n  // the simulation time to bleed, dry, edge-darken before we consider\n  // shutting it off.\n  if (framesSincePaint < PAINT_GRACE_FRAMES) return true;\n  if (simIsIdle) return false;\n  // Past the grace period and not currently idle: check periodically\n  // whether the canvas has settled.\n  if (framesSinceIdleCheck < IDLE_CHECK_INTERVAL) return true;\n  framesSinceIdleCheck = 0;\n  const avgWet = lastTotalWet / N;\n  const avgSusp = lastTotalSuspended / N;\n  if (avgWet < IDLE_WET_PER_CELL && avgSusp < IDLE_SUSPENDED_PER_CELL) {\n    simIsIdle = true;\n    return false;\n  }\n  return true;\n}\n\nfunction loop() {\n  applyPendingPaint();\n  aiStep();\n  framesSincePaint++;\n  framesSinceIdleCheck++;\n\n  if (shouldRunSim()) {\n    simStep();\n    simStep();\n    render();\n  }\n  // Bars still update every frame \u2014 they cost N reads but maintain the\n  // totals that the idle check depends on, and they let the bars finish\n  // their CSS transitions cleanly when the sim shuts off.\n  updateBars();\n  requestAnimationFrame(loop);\n}\nloop();",
      "old_str": "function loop() {\n  applyPendingPaint();\n  aiStep();\n  // Two sim sub-steps per frame for a livelier flow\n  simStep();\n  simStep();\n  render();\n  updateDrynessBar();\n  updatePigmentBar();\n  requestAnimationFrame(loop);\n}\nloop();",

      "new_str": "// ============================================================\n// IDLE SKIP \u2014 sim/render skipping when canvas is settled\n// ============================================================\n// Most of a typical session, nothing is happening: the canvas is dry,\n// no pigment is moving, no input is arriving. Running simStep + render\n// every frame in that state burns CPU for no visible change. This\n// state machine detects when the sim has truly settled and skips the\n// hot path until something changes.\n//\n// Wake conditions (anything that mutates state must call markCanvasActive):\n//   \u2022 paintAt   \u2014 user or AI applies paint\n//   \u2022 rewet     \u2014 global re-wet button\n//   \u2022 resetSim  \u2014 Reset button\n//\n// Sleep conditions (all checked together once framesSincePaint > grace):\n//   \u2022 Average wet per cell below a small threshold\n//   \u2022 Average suspended pigment per cell below a small threshold\n// Deposited pigment (d[]) is dry and won't change without water input,\n// so we don't need it in the idle check.\n//\n// The totals lastTotalWet / lastTotalSuspended are maintained by\n// updateBars (which runs every frame), so the idle check itself is O(1).\n// State (framesSincePaint, simIsIdle, totals) is declared up near the\n// state arrays so paintAt / resetSim / rewet can call markCanvasActive\n// during init without TDZ errors.\n\nconst PAINT_GRACE_FRAMES = 60;        // 1 sec of forced sim after every paint\nconst IDLE_CHECK_INTERVAL = 30;\nconst IDLE_WET_PER_CELL = 0.025;      // matches evaporate's dry-out threshold\nconst IDLE_SUSPENDED_PER_CELL = 0.0005;\n\nfunction shouldRunSim() {\n  // Always run during the grace period after any paint event \u2014 gives\n  // the simulation time to bleed, dry, edge-darken before we consider\n  // shutting it off.\n  if (framesSincePaint < PAINT_GRACE_FRAMES) return true;\n  if (simIsIdle) return false;\n  // Past the grace period and not currently idle: check periodically\n  // whether the canvas has settled.\n  if (framesSinceIdleCheck < IDLE_CHECK_INTERVAL) return true;\n  framesSinceIdleCheck = 0;\n  const avgWet = lastTotalWet / N;\n  const avgSusp = lastTotalSuspended / N;\n  if (avgWet < IDLE_WET_PER_CELL && avgSusp < IDLE_SUSPENDED_PER_CELL) {\n    simIsIdle = true;\n    return false;\n  }\n  return true;\n}\n\nfunction loop() {\n  applyPendingPaint();\n  aiStep();\n  framesSincePaint++;\n  framesSinceIdleCheck++;\n\n  if (shouldRunSim()) {\n    simStep();\n    simStep();\n    render();\n  }\n  // Bars still update every frame \u2014 they cost N reads but maintain the\n  // totals that the idle check depends on, and they let the bars finish\n  // their CSS transitions cleanly when the sim shuts off.\n  updateBars();\n  requestAnimationFrame(loop);\n}\nloop();",

For pressure decay, I'm realizing that dry cells should have zero pressure anyway since edge darkening only affects wet cells and pressure naturally decays over time, so multiplying zero by the decay factor is harmless.\n\nThe real win would be inlining the decay into the velocity loop to eliminate a separate O(N) sweep entirely. But the velocity loop only covers interior cells, not boundaries, so I'd need to handle boundary pressure decay separately to keep the behavior consistent.\n\nActually, the decay loop is already so cheap\u2014just a single multiply with no neighbor reads\u2014that it's probably not worth the complexity. I'll leave it as is.\n\nNow looking at the wetBinary computation in applyEdgeDarkening: it's a separate pass that converts the wet array to binary values, but I can't fuse it into the box blur since the blur needs the entire array initialized first. The real opportunity might be fusing the wetBinary computation with the evaporate step that runs before it in the next frame, since evaporate finishes writing the wet array completely.\n\nBut wait\u2014within a single simStep, applyEdgeDarkening runs first and evaporate runs last, so fusing them would shift the wetBinary computation by one frame, meaning edge darkening would use a stale wet mask. That's a behavior change, even if it's probably imperceptible. The wetBinary pass itself is already one of the cheapest operations\u2014just N reads and N writes\u2014so it's not worth the complexity.\n\nActually, I notice wetBinary is typed as Float32Array based on the .set calls. For the box blur, it adds wetBinary values into a sum, and with a 4-pixel radius kernel, the maximum sum is 9, which fits fine. I could optimize by using Uint8Array instead since the values are binary, but the implicit conversion during floating-point reads probably wouldn't be faster than Float32Array anyway. I'll skip that micro-optimization and focus on testing what I have.\n\nBefore moving forward, let me check if I'm missing anything important by reviewing the render function for other optimization opportunities. Actually, I should also precompute the Laplacian kernel offline...\n\nWait, I'm looking at the movePigment function and those offset calculations like `i - 1`, `i + 1`, `i - GW`, `i + GW` are already computed once per cell, so they're cheap. I could hoist `i - GW` out of the inner loop to save one subtraction, but V8 optimizes that trivially anyway. Let me ship what I have and measure the performance.\n\nOh, there's one more issue \u2014 I'm caching `g[0]`, `g[1]`, `g[2]` into local variables inside transferPigment, but those arrays get reassigned in evaporate, so I need to be careful about stale references.\n\nActually, no \u2014 they're modified in-place with `g_tmp[k].set(g[k])`, which copies data into the existing typed array without changing the reference itself. So the locals stay valid.\n\nNow I'm checking the idle-skip behavior: when render is skipped, the canvas doesn't get cleared because `ctx.clearRect` only runs inside render(). The previous frame's pixels stay visible on the canvas. But I think I already added `ctx.clearRect` in v0.6.1, so I need to verify that's still there.\n\nThere's a potential issue with theme or paper changes while the sim is idle \u2014 the canvas might show the old painted content against a new background, though the transparent areas should correctly display the new body color. I need to wake the simulation when the paper color changes so the canvas re-renders with the new substrate, even if it's been idle for a while. Calling markCanvasActive() will trigger the render loop for enough frames to composite the painted pigment against the new paper color without actually changing the simulation state.\n\nActually, a cleaner approach is to just call render() directly in setPaperColor after updating the color \u2014 no need to wake the whole sim. For the other toggles like \"Transparent\" and \"Dark,\" those are purely CSS-driven based on the paper luminance, so they don't need re-rendering. The edge darkening toggle is trickier though \u2014 that affects simulation behavior, so if we're idle from a previous darkened session and the user toggles it, future paint strokes will use the new setting.\n\nSince render() is a function declaration and gets hoisted, I can safely call it from setPaperColor.",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "v0.7 is the perf pass. Five optimizations, no visualization changes \u2014 every output should be pixel-identical to v0.6.11, just produced with less CPU.\n\n**1. Idle-skip \u2014 the big one.** Most of a typical session, nothing's happening: canvas is dry, no pigment moving, no input. v0.6 ran the full simStep\u00d72 + render every frame regardless. v0.7 detects when the canvas has settled (avg wet < 0.025, avg suspended pigment < 0.0005) and skips simStep + render entirely until something wakes it. Wake conditions: any `paintAt`, `rewet`, or `resetSim` call resets a counter and forces 1 second of simulation before idle-checking resumes. The idle check itself is O(1) because it reads totals already maintained by the bar update. Effect: when canvas is settled and AI is off, CPU usage drops to roughly just the bar update + animation frame overhead.\n\n**2. Fused diffusion in `movePigment`.** The pigment diffusion was 3 separate full passes through every cell (one per pigment), each touching `wet[i]` independently. Fused into 1 pass that handles all three Laplacians inline per cell. Saves 2N iterations + redundant `wet[i]` checks + better cache reuse on neighbor reads. The math is bit-identical \u2014 same Laplacian, same `PIGMENT_DIFFUSION` coefficient, same skip-on-dry guard.\n\n**3. Fused `transferPigment`.** Same pattern: was 3 passes (one per pigment), now 1 pass with all three \u00a74.5 adsorption/desorption updates inline. The pigment-specific constants (density, staining, granulation) get hoisted to local vars before the loop so V8 keeps them in registers rather than refetching from the `PIGMENTS` array per iteration. Per-cell `wet[i]` and `paperH[i]` reads also now happen once instead of 3\u00d7.\n\n**4. Fused bar updates.** Was two separate O(N) passes \u2014 one summed `wet[i]`, the other summed `g[k][i] + d[k][i]` across pigments. Now one pass produces all three totals (`tWet`, `tSusp`, `tPig`). The first two are stored to `lastTotalWet` / `lastTotalSuspended` for the idle check, the third updates the bar fill + readout. Halves bar-update cost and makes the idle check piggyback on work that already happens.\n\n**5. Box blur with split inner loops.** The original had `Math.max(0, ...)` / `Math.min(GW-1, ...)` calls on every inner iteration to clamp the sliding-window indices. With `EDGE_KERNEL_LARGE = 20`, the middle region of each row is roughly 95% of the iterations \u2014 and for that middle region the clamps are always no-ops. The new version splits each pass into three regions: left edge (where the subtract index needs clamping), middle (no clamps, the hot loop), right edge (where the add index needs clamping). Identical output, much tighter code in the middle loop.\n\n**Honesty about what I didn't do:**\n\n- **Worker / OffscreenCanvas.** Would get the sim off the main thread, biggest architectural win, but it's a real refactor and probably belongs to v1.0 / library extraction.\n- **WebGL/WebGPU port.** Same \u2014 huge speedup potential but full rewrite.\n- **Sparse activity tracking.** Tile-based active-region tracking would dwarf everything above for partially painted canvases, but it's invasive and risky for a \"no visualization changes\" pass.\n- **Velocity buffer swap.** `updateVelocity` does `u.set(u_new); v.set(v_new);` \u2014 a typed-array copy of N floats per call. The proper fix is to alias-swap pointer references, but `u` and `v` are `const` and many downstream functions reference them by name. The `.set()` is well-optimized in modern browsers anyway, so it's not bad \u2014 just not great.\n- **Render fast-path expansion.** The current render has a `xt < 0.004` fast-path that skips KM. I considered raising that threshold to skip more cells, but at higher values you'd start seeing visible discontinuities at low-pigment regions. Left alone for visual fidelity.\n\n**Expected impact:** when canvas is fully idle and AI is off, near-zero CPU. During active painting, simStep work is reduced by roughly the fusion savings \u2014 call it 20-30% on the diffusion + transfer passes, and another 10-15% off edge darkening from the box blur tweak. Real-world feel should be: paint sessions feel noticeably less stuttery during heavy AI activity, and the system fan no longer kicks on if you leave the tab open.",
    "citations": []
  }

      "new_str": "// Re-wet: raise water saturation back to a high baseline everywhere, and\n// lift a fraction of deposited pigment back into suspension so dried\n// strokes become mobile again. The \u00a74.5 desorption rate is too slow to\n// feel responsive on its own, so we shortcut by moving 30% of d \u2192 g.\nconst REWET_LEVEL = 0.85;\nconst REWET_LIFT  = 0.30;\nfunction rewet() {\n  markCanvasActive();\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    if (wet[i] < REWET_LEVEL) wet[i] = REWET_LEVEL;\n    for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n      const dval = d[k][i];\n      if (dval < 0.001) continue;\n      const lift = dval * REWET_LIFT;\n      d[k][i] = dval - lift;\n      const nv = g[k][i] + lift;\n      g[k][i] = nv > MAX_PIGMENT ? We render+updateBars explicitly so the damp-sheen disappears\n// from unpainted cells and the dryness bar fills immediately, without\n// needing to wake the sim for 60 grace frames just to flush a visual\n// that won't change again.\nfunction dryPaper() {\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    // Settle suspended \u2192 deposited (same rule evaporate uses)\n    for (let k = 0; k < 3; k++) {\n      let nd = d[k][i] + g[k][i];\n      if (nd > MAX_PIGMENT) nd = MAX_PIGMENT;\n      d[k][i] = nd;\n      g[k][i] = 0;\n    }\n    wet[i] = 0;\n    u[i] = 0; v[i] = 0;\n    pressure[i] = 0;\n  }\n  render();\n  updateBars();\n}",

    "text": "v0.7.1. Dry sits next to Re-wet with the same momentary-flash treatment.\n\nThe implementation mirrors `rewet` structurally but inverts every action:\n\n- **Rewet** raises `wet[i]` to 0.85, lifts 30% of deposited pigment back into suspension.\n- **Dry** zeros `wet[i]`, settles all suspended pigment down to deposited, and zeros velocity + pressure.\n\nThe pigment-settling step (`g \u2192 d`) is exactly the same dump rule `evaporate` uses when a cell crosses below 0.025 wet on its own \u2014 I just apply it unconditionally to every cell. So the visual end-state is identical to what natural evaporation would eventually produce, just achieved instantly. Total pigment is conserved across the dry.\n\n**Two small differences from `rewet`:** I didn't call `markCanvasActive`, and I do explicitly invoke `render()` and `updateBars()` at the end of `dryPaper`. Reasoning: after drying, the sim has nothing left to simulate \u2014 every cell will fail the `wet[i] < 0.04` check in subsequent simSteps. Waking the sim for the standard 60-frame grace period would just waste cycles iterating dry cells that all early-exit. Instead, I push the one render that's needed (to remove the subtle damp-sheen from unpainted cells, a `wet * 0.018` brightness boost that goes away when wet hits zero) and the one bar update that's needed (to fill the dryness indicator to 100%). The sim stays in whatever idle/active state it was in.\n\nThe dryness bar pinning to 100% is the main user-visible feedback. The painted regions visually stay the same (the render compositor sums `g + d` for thickness so g\u2194d transitions don't change KM output), and the bare paper gets very slightly less bright \u2014 perceptible side-by-side but a one-frame transition. The 250ms CSS animation on the bar fill provides the \"felt\" feedback.\n\n**Interactions worth noting:**\n\n- **Dry + paint:** painting on freshly-dried paper works fine. The `paintAt` adds water and pigment, the cell becomes wet again, the sim resumes. So you can use Dry as a \"lock in this state, start fresh\" operation.\n- **Dry + Pause drying:** if \"Pause drying\" is on, the sim's natural evaporation is disabled, but `dryPaper` runs anyway \u2014 it doesn't go through `evaporate`. So you can flip-flop: paint with bleed (drying paused), dry instantly, paint again. Useful for layered effects.\n- **Dry + Re-wet:** click Dry then Re-wet and you'll get a re-mobilized version of the painting, with 30% of the now-deposited pigment lifted back into suspension. This is a way to \"set\" pigment and then \"wash\" it.",
    "citations": []
  }

  {
    "start_timestamp": "2026-05-11T02:31:17.354924Z",
    "stop_timestamp": "2026-05-11T02:33:21.733264Z",
    "flags": null,
    "type": "thinking",
    "thinking": " So the user wants pigment to gradually fade away from the canvas over time, essentially the opposite of accumulation \u2014 everything should eventually return to blank paper. I need to figure out what fades (both suspended and deposited pigment), how quickly it happens, and what timeframe makes sense for \"gradual\" and \"subtle.\"\n\nThe key insight is that this fade should happen independently of whether the canvas is idle or actively being painted on \u2014 otherwise the pigment would only disappear when the user is working. I'll make this a default behavior with a tunable decay constant rather than a toggle, since the user asked to \"add\" it as a feature.\n\nFor the math, I'm using a simple multiplicative decay model where the pigment values get multiplied by a factor slightly less than 1 each step, creating exponential decay with a calculable half-life.\n\nWorking through the timing: with a factor of 0.9995, the half-life is about 1386 simulation steps, which translates to roughly 11.5 seconds at 60fps. For something more gradual on a minute scale, I need to adjust the factor\u2014a 2-minute half-life would use approximately 0.99995, while 5 or 10 minutes would require even slower decay factors like 0.99998 or 0.99999.\n\nI'm leaning toward the 2-minute range for a subtle, gradual fade that feels natural without disappearing too quickly.\n\nNow I'm thinking about what happens with very small pigment values over time. The advection and evaporation logic have thresholds that skip processing when values drop below certain limits, and the renderer treats anything below 0.004 as blank paper anyway. But I should probably zero out values that get too small to prevent them from blocking the idle check indefinitely\u2014otherwise those tiny non-zero remnants would keep the system thinking there's still activity.\n\nActually, looking at the idle check more carefully, it only cares about suspended pigment, not deposited pigment, so the fade behavior only affects visuals. The real issue is that if I fade pigment only during simulation steps, then when the canvas goes idle and the sim stops running, the pigment won't fade at all\u2014which defeats the purpose of having a fade in the first place.\n\nI'm weighing three approaches: running fade every frame regardless of idle status, forcing the sim to stay running so fade always happens, or running fade periodically even when idle. The cleanest option is to move fade outside the simulation step and run it every frame, since it's cheap compared to the full simulation. But that creates a new problem\u2014if fade is modifying the pigment arrays every frame but the render isn't running during idle, the visual will be stale. So I'd either need to render every frame too (which defeats idle-skip), render every few frames to show visible stepping, or skip idle optimization entirely. The fade is meant to be subtle, so running it every couple of seconds would mean each step needs to be quite aggressive to be noticeable.\n\nActually, I'm realizing the real issue: the idle check only looks at water and suspended pigment, not deposited pigment. So a dry painting sitting on the canvas counts as idle even though it's visually present and needs to fade. I need to handle fade and re-render manually for these idle-but-visible states.\n\nFor the fade implementation, I'm creating a separate function that applies multiplicative decay to both the wet and dry pigment arrays. With N around 572k pixels, that's roughly 3.4 million multiplies per frame, which should be negligible on modern hardware.\n\nBut there's a tradeoff: if I want the fade to be visible during idle periods, I'd need to keep render() running every frame anyway, which defeats the purpose of skipping simulation steps. So instead, I'm exploring a batched approach where fading happens less frequently\u2014say every 30 frames\u2014and then triggers a re-render, which should be much more efficient overall.\n\nImplementing fade tick logic... The fade happens at 0.5Hz, so each step is subtle enough that the steppiness isn't noticeable. Setting the render flag ensures the display updates even when the simulation is idle, and the computational cost averages out to just 0.2N multiplies per frame across the 30-frame interval.\n\nApproach C would integrate fading directly into the simulation step, but that breaks the whole point\u2014idle canvases wouldn't fade at all without user interaction. So I'm down to A or B. Option A is simpler and always runs the fade, adding 6N multiplies per frame, which becomes the dominant cost when idle. When the canvas is idle, I'm looking at roughly 12N total work per frame between the fade and bar updates, which is significantly less than the alternative.\n\nBut let me reconsider the actual costs here. When idle, render is doing the heavy lifting with all the math operations\u2014around 30N ops or more. So the total idle cost is closer to 40N per frame. Compared to an active state where I'm running simStep twice plus render, that's roughly 60N+ per frame. Option A still wins, and the simplicity makes it the better choice.\n\nThough now I'm wondering if I can optimize the fade itself. The per-frame fade is so subtle that it's imperceptible\u2014the visual changes only accumulate over time. What if I render less frequently during fade, maybe at 2Hz instead of every frame, and let the fade math run at a higher rate to maintain the same half-life? That way I could skip most of the expensive render calls while still tracking the decay correctly.\n\nActually, I don't even need to apply the fade every frame. If I apply it every 6 frames but use a stronger fade factor to compensate, the result at every 6th frame stays identical. So I could batch the fade and render together at 10Hz instead of 60Hz, which cuts the overhead significantly.\n\nImplementing a fade-and-render function that only runs every 6 frames...\n\nCalculating the performance cost: at 10Hz I'm doing 60N operations per second for fade, 300N for rendering, and 360N for bar updates, totaling 720N ops/sec when idle. That's double the current v0.7 idle cost of 360N ops/sec, so the fade does add overhead, but it's manageable.\n\nI'm reconsidering the architecture though \u2014 instead of separate fade and update functions, I could fuse them since they're both iterating over the same cells anyway. Or I could run the fade every frame and use a \"needsRender\" flag to control when the canvas actually updates, letting the main loop's idle logic decide when to draw.\n\nBut that brings me back to the core tension: fading requires rendering, which means I can't truly idle-skip. So I'm settling on a middle ground \u2014 run fade and render together at a fixed lower frequency like 10Hz while letting everything else idle-skip when nothing's happening. For the implementation, I'm thinking of adding a fade decay constant that gives pigment a reasonable half-life, accounting for how throttling affects the simulation step rate. ach #2:\n- Active: fade runs in simStep (twice per frame = 120/sec at 60fps)\n- Idle: fade runs at 10Hz with a 12x larger step\n\nTo get consistent half-life regardless of active/idle, the per-call factor changes. Or we can use wall-clock time.\n\nHmm actually maybe simpler: define fade in terms of wall-clock half-life. Compute the appropriate per-call factor based on elapsed time since last call.\n\nBut that's complex. Let me just pick a simple per-call factor and accept that fade rate varies slightly between active and idle.\n\nActually even simpler: only fade when canvas is fully settled (dry + minimal pigment movement). I.e., link fade to drying.\n\nBut the user said \"gradual fade across the entire canvas so the painting fades away\" \u2014 this should happen on dry pigment (d[]) which is what makes up the painting. Suspended pigment (g[]) is transient.\n\nWhat if we ONLY fade d[]? Then suspended pigment naturally settles into deposited via transferPigment, deposited pigment gradually decays over time, and eventually the canvas appears blank. This is conceptually cleaner: lifting of pigment affects laid-down paint, not in-flight pigment. And fading only d[] doesn't interfere with the active simulation since that's mostly about g[] dynamics. The cost is just 3N multiplies per fade application.\n\nNow I'm thinking about where the pigment goes when we fade d[]. Physically it's lifted off the paper \u2014 we could discard it entirely, or lift it back into suspension so the fade appears as a slow re-suspending with the pigment bleeding around before settling again. The latter is more physically grounded but requires water to be present, otherwise the pigment would immediately dump back into d[]. If we just decay d[] without conservation, it's like the pigment evaporated into the air \u2014 physically odd but visually it gives us the gradual fade we want.\n\nFor the user's goal of a painting that fades away entirely, simple non-conservative decay of d[] is the right approach. I'll run this fade every frame in the main loop, applying a decay factor to d[] across all cells \u2014 it's only 3N multiplies per frame, which at 60fps across 572k cells is negligible overhead. Now I need to figure out the rendering side.\n\nI'm deciding to run fade at a fixed interval (every 6 frames at 60fps = 10Hz) and force a render whenever fade runs, so idle-skip still skips the simulation but renders periodically to show the decay. Setting up the fade loop with a decay factor and frame counter...\n\nImplementing the fade loop...\n\nThe key insight is that when the sim is running normally, the fade changes get rendered automatically with each frame, but when idle we need an explicit render call after fading to show the pigment decay.\n\nRefining the loop structure...\n\nThe approach tracks whether fade actually modified pigment values, so we only render when either the simulation runs or pigment has faded. In idle mode, this gives us fade and render happening at 10Hz while skipping the expensive simulation steps, bringing the per-frame cost down to roughly render overhead plus bar updates plus amortized fade work.\n\nLooking at the actual numbers, idle-with-fade runs around 2.2G operations per second compared to 3.4G when fully active, so we're cutting the cost to about a third. That's meaningful savings, though not as dramatic as skipping rendering entirely. I'm considering whether dropping the fade frequency to 2Hz instead would be worth the tradeoff\u2014it'd slash idle costs significantly, but the visual stepping from larger fade increments might become noticeable to users.\n\nLet me start with 10Hz as a reasonable default and let the user adjust if needed. On the question of whether fading should apply to suspended pigment in the g[] array\u2014I think the answer is no. Suspended pigment represents paint still flowing during the drying process, and fading that would make fresh strokes lose vibrancy too quickly. The nice behavior here is that fresh paint stays vivid while only the settled, dried pigment in d[] gradually fades, which feels more natural.\n\nFor optimization, I could add a threshold to zero out very small values in d[] to save computation, but that introduces a branch per cell which probably isn't worth the cleanup unless there are other places that would benefit from it.\n\nNow I'm thinking about whether to add a toggle for the fade behavior. It makes sense to include one for consistency with the other toggles like \"Pause drying,\" giving users control over whether fading is active. Based on the request for a gradual fade that eventually clears the canvas, I should default fading to on and label the toggle as \"Pause fading\" so it follows the same pattern as the existing controls.\n\nBut looking at the controls row, it's already getting crowded with Edge darkening, Pause drying, AI painter, Transparent, plus the action buttons. Adding another toggle might clutter things further, so maybe I'll just make fading always-on for now without a toggle\u2014they can ask for one if they want more control.\n\nImplementing pigment fade... = 0.999423\n\nFor 5 minutes (300 sec) half-life @ 10Hz: f = 0.99977\nFor 10 minutes (600 sec) @ 10Hz: f = 0.99988\n\nThese fade factors are really close to 1, so let me think about this differently \u2014 what matters is when the painting becomes imperceptible. If I want it mostly faded after 30 minutes but still slightly visible at 10 minutes, a 5-minute half-life gives me the right progression: half strength at 5 min, a quarter at 10 min, barely visible by 20-30 minutes.\n\nSo I'll go with FADE_FACTOR = 0.99977 for a 10Hz update rate. To make this more maintainable, I'm parameterizing the calculation...\n\nCalculating fade factor from parameters...\n\nNow I'm reconsidering whether fade should pause when drying is paused. They're conceptually different\u2014drying is short-term water evaporation while fade is long-term decay\u2014so I'll keep them decoupled. But actually, if fade runs constantly in the background, users working on a painting for extended periods would want to pause it, so maybe I should reconsider the toggle approach.\n\nOn second thought, I'll skip the toggle for now. The user asked for gradual fade as a feature, and adding a toggle adds UI clutter. If they need to disable it, they can ask. Keeping it simple is the right call.\n\nNow I'm implementing the fade mechanism with tunable constants at the top of the section, applying a slow multiplicative decay to the deposited pigment while leaving suspended pigment untouched so wet strokes stay vibrant until they settle.\n\nWriting the fade implementation... Writing the main loop... Refactoring the animation loop structure...\n\nI'm moving the render call outside the simulation block so it fires whenever the sim runs OR when the fade updates, preventing double renders. The loop now checks if the simulation should step, handles fade timing separately, then consolidates all rendering into a single conditional call. The fade mechanism won't interfere with the idle detection since it only affects pigment values, not the wet or suspended state that triggers the sim skip. Once fade drives all pigment to near-zero, the canvas becomes both idle and blank \u2014 the bars will show minimal values, fade will keep multiplying near-zero by the decay factor, and render will display the same blank state every six frames.\n\nI'm realizing the repeated rendering of an unchanged canvas is inefficient. I could track the total pigment from the last render and skip rendering if it hasn't changed meaningfully, though fade does alter values slightly so I'd need a threshold. Better yet, once total pigment drops below a visual threshold like 0.0005 per particle, I can stop applying fade entirely since the canvas is already imperceptibly blank \u2014 the state stays frozen at near-zero without needing further decay calculations.\n\nAdding a pigment threshold check...\n\nActually, I should track just the deposited pigment separately rather than mixing it with the growth value, since I only want to fade based on what's actually visible on the canvas. iplies/sec. Pretty cheap. Rendering every 6 frames when idle costs around 190M ops/sec, which is heavier than the fade step but manageable. When actively painting, the combined render and simulation cost hits about 3.4G ops/sec, so the fade adds only about 6% overhead\u2014negligible. When idle, the total jumps from just bar updates to around 310N/sec, which is a substantial relative increase but still low in absolute terms. This seems worth shipping.\n\nOne last thing to check: the comments mention \"pigment fade\"\n\n\u2014I could use \"fugitive\" since that's the actual art term for pigments that fade over time, but it's pretty obscure. Better to stick with \"pigment fade\" or just \"fading\" so it's clear to everyone.\n\nNow implementing.",

Reason: wet, in-flight pigment should look vibrant until it settles; fading suspended pigment would make fresh strokes look \"weak\" the moment they're laid down. Once paint dries into `d[]`, it's part of the long-term painting and starts to fade.\n\n**When does it run?** Every frame, regardless of `simIsIdle`. This is the subtle interaction with v0.7's idle-skip: the canvas can be sim-idle (water gone, pigment settled) while the painting is still slowly fading. So fade has to run *outside* `simStep`, and we have to trigger `render()` when fade changes things while the sim is asleep.\n\n**How fast?** Set a target half-life in seconds (5 min by default) and derive the per-tick factor from it. Easier to reason about than tuning a multiplier directly.\n\n**What about cost?** Fade itself is cheap (3N multiplies per tick). The expensive part is having to re-render at the fade tick rate even when sim is idle, since previously idle-skip skipped render too. Throttling fade ticks to 10Hz keeps idle CPU reasonable.\n\nLet me also factor out `render()` from inside the `shouldRunSim` block so a fade tick can trigger it independently:",

The factor derives from FADE_HALFLIFE_SEC for tuning convenience. fadeStep does a single multiplicative decay pass over the three deposited pigment arrays.\n\nThe loop is restructured: shouldRunSim and the fade tick decision are independent, both can request a render, and render() is called once at the end if either triggered. This is cleaner than the v0.7 structure where render was nested inside the sim block.",
      "new_str": "// ============================================================\n// PIGMENT FADE \u2014 gradual lifting of deposited pigment over time\n// ============================================================\n// Multiplicative decay applied to d[k][i] across all cells at a fixed\n// tick rate, regardless of simulation activity. Paintings slowly fade\n// to bare paper over minutes. Suspended pigment (g[]) is intentionally\n// NOT faded \u2014 wet strokes stay vibrant until they settle into d[],\n// then begin contributing to the slow fade.\n//\n// The factor is derived from a target half-life in seconds for tuning\n// convenience: set FADE_HALFLIFE_SEC, the per-tick multiplier follows.\nconst FADE_HALFLIFE_SEC = 300;       // 5 min: half of d[] gone every 5 min\nconst FADE_TICK_INTERVAL = 6;        // 10 Hz at 60 fps; trade CPU for smoothness\nconst FADE_FACTOR = Math.pow(0.5, FADE_TICK_INTERVAL / 60 / FADE_HALFLIFE_SEC);\n// Don't run fade when there's essentially nothing left to fade \u2014\n// avoids waking render() forever to multiply already-zero values.\nconst FADE_SKIP_BELOW = 0.001;       // avg pigment per cell\n\nlet framesSinceFade = 0;\n\nfunction fadeStep() {\n  const f = FADE_FACTOR;\n  const d0 = d[0], d1 = d[1], d2 = d[2];\n  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {\n    d0[i] *= f;\n    d1[i] *= f;\n    d2[i] *= f;\n  }\n}\n\nfunction loop() {\n  applyPendingPaint();\n  aiStep();\n  framesSincePaint++;\n  framesSinceIdleCheck++;\n  framesSinceFade++;\n\n  const runSim = shouldRunSim();\n  if (runSim) {\n    simStep();\n    simStep();\n  }\n\n  // Fade tick: 10 Hz regardless of sim activity, but skipped once the\n  // canvas has effectively faded to blank \u2014 no point spending cycles\n  // multiplying near-zeroes.\n  let didFade = false;\n  if (framesSinceFade >= FADE_TICK_INTERVAL) {\n    framesSinceFade = 0;\n    if (currentAvgPigment > FADE_SKIP_BELOW) {\n      fadeStep();\n      didFade = true;\n    }\n  }\n\n  // Render if EITHER the sim ran (state changed via simStep) OR the\n  // fade tick changed d[] while sim was idle. When both are false the\n  // canvas is unchanged frame-to-frame; the existing rendered pixels\n  // stay correct.\n  if (runSim || didFade) render();\n\n  // Bars still update every frame \u2014 they cost N reads but maintain the\n  // totals that the idle check depends on, and they let the bars finish\n  // their CSS transitions cleanly when the sim shuts off.\n  updateBars();\n  requestAnimationFrame(loop);\n}\nloop();",

    "text": "v0.7.2. A painting will roughly halve every 5 minutes and reach ~1% of its original strength after 33 minutes \u2014 so visible for a fairly long time, but eventually back to bare paper if left alone.\n\nA few specifics worth knowing:\n\n**Only deposited pigment fades.** Suspended pigment (`g[]`) is untouched; it represents wet, in-flight pigment that hasn't settled yet. So a fresh stroke stays vivid while it's bleeding around. As it dries via `transferPigment` and `evaporate`, the pigment moves into `d[]` and joins the fade pool. This means an actively-being-painted canvas barely fades \u2014 the fade is mostly a property of the dried, completed parts.\n\n**Half-life is set in seconds for tuning convenience.** `FADE_HALFLIFE_SEC = 300` is the only number to touch if you want it slower or faster. The per-tick multiplier is derived from it via `0.5^(tick_interval / 60 / halflife_sec)`. Set it to 600 for 10-min half-life, 60 for 1-min, etc.\n\n**Tick rate is 10Hz, not 60Hz.** The fade math could equivalently apply 1/6 as much decay every frame, but applying a slightly larger decay every 6th frame is six times cheaper without any perceptible difference. It also means the \"force a render after fade tick\" only fires 10\u00d7 per second when the sim is otherwise idle, rather than 60\u00d7 \u2014 preserving most of v0.7's idle-skip benefit.\n\n**The render is the expensive part, not the fade.** `fadeStep` itself is just 3N multiplies \u2014 tiny. The trickier part is that fading means the canvas changes even when `simIsIdle` is true, so we have to call `render()` on fade ticks. That's the cost. The `FADE_SKIP_BELOW = 0.001` threshold means once average pigment per cell falls below visible threshold, fade stops entirely \u2014 at that point the canvas is effectively blank and we let it stay there without spending cycles multiplying near-zeroes forever.\n\n**Loop structure change.** v0.7 had `render()` nested inside the `shouldRunSim` block. I had to pull it out so a fade tick can request a render independently. Both `runSim` and `didFade` now independently decide; render is called once at the end if either is true. When both are false (canvas idle and pigment too low to fade), nothing renders, which keeps idle CPU truly minimal.\n\n**Edge cases handled:**\n- Re-wet, Dry, Reset, paint events don't need any special fade handling \u2014 fade just keeps running in the background regardless.\n- The Dry button settles all `g[] \u2192 d[]`, so anything you instantly dry will start fading. That's correct.\n- The Pause-drying toggle doesn't affect fade. They're orthogonal \u2014 drying is about water, fading is about pigment.\n- If pigment is added back (paint, AI) while the canvas was below `FADE_SKIP_BELOW`, the next bar update raises `currentAvgPigment` and fade resumes automatically.",
    "citations": []
  }
]

Suspended\n// pigment (g[]) is intentionally NOT faded \u2014 wet strokes stay vibrant\n// until they settle into d[], then begin contributing to the fade.\n//\n// The half-life is mutable so the front-end slider can adjust it at\n// runtime. fadeFactor is derived from it and recomputed on every\n// change via setFadeHalfLifeMs.\nconst FADE_TICK_INTERVAL = 6;        // 10 Hz at 60 fps; trade CPU for smoothness\nconst FADE_DEFAULT_MS = 4000;        // 4 s default half-life\nconst FADE_MIN_MS = 500;             // slider min\nconst FADE_MAX_MS = 30000;           // slider max\n// Don't run fade when there's essentially nothing left to fade \u2014\n// avoids waking render() forever to multiply already-zero values.\nconst FADE_SKIP_BELOW = 0.001;       // avg pigment per cell\n\nlet fadeEnabled = false;             // toggled by \"Fade painting\" button\nlet fadeHalfLifeMs = FADE_DEFAULT_MS;\nlet fadeFactor = Math.pow(0.5, FADE_TICK_INTERVAL / 60 / (fadeHalfLifeMs / 1000));\nlet framesSinceFade = 0;\n\nfunction setFadeHalfLifeMs(ms) {\n  // Clamp defensively in case the slider sends something out of range\n  if (ms < FADE_MIN_MS) ms = FADE_MIN_MS;\n  else if (ms > FADE_MAX_MS) ms = FADE_MAX_MS;\n  fadeHalfLifeMs = ms;\n  fadeFactor = Math.pow(0.5, FADE_TICK_INTERVAL / 60 / (ms / 1000));\n}",

    "type": "text",
    "text": "v0.7.5. The fade slider sits on its own row beneath the brush slider, hidden until you turn on \"Fade painting\" \u2014 then it appears with range 500ms to 30000ms, default 4000ms, 100ms steps.\n\nThe math behind it is one `Math.pow` recomputed every time the slider moves (continuous `input` event). The factor formula stays the same: `0.5^(tickInterval / 60 / halfLifeSec)` \u2014 just with the half-life expressed in milliseconds at the UI layer and divided by 1000 internally.\n\nA few small UX notes:\n\n**Slider value persists across toggle on/off.** If you tune to 2500ms, turn fade off for a while, then turn it back on, you keep 2500ms. The slider element is always in the DOM (just hidden), so its value sticks.\n\n**Linear scale, not logarithmic.** 30000ms isn't really thirty times more useful than 1000ms perceptually, but the slider treats them equally. Practical consequence: the bottom third of the slider (500ms\u201310000ms) is where you'll spend most of the time, since that's where the painting actually visibly fades on human timescales. The upper end (20000\u201330000ms) is \"barely fading at all.\" If the linear feel becomes annoying I can switch to a log scale, but for tuning between 500ms and ~10000ms it's perfectly usable as-is.\n\n**Clamping is defensive.** The slider's HTML `min`/`max` already constrain the value, but `setFadeHalfLifeMs` clamps internally too \u2014 that way if you (or a future caller) sets the value programmatically with an out-of-range number, the fade factor stays sane.\n\n**The label updates from the clamped value, not the raw input.** So even if someone tries to set 999999ms, the displayed text caps at 30000.\n\nThe constant `FADE_TICK_INTERVAL = 6` (10Hz update) and the skip threshold `FADE_SKIP_BELOW = 0.001` stay hardcoded. If you wanted to expose those too they'd be straightforward additions, but they're more \"implementation\" than \"feel\" \u2014 adjusting tick rate would mainly trade CPU for visual smoothness rather than change the artistic result.",
    "citations": []
  }
]
